title: Soda farls
subtitle: Northern Irish griddle-cooked soda bread in quarters
excerpt: Soda farls are Northern Irish flat soda bread cooked on a dry griddle rather than baked in the oven. Buttermilk and bicarbonate of soda lift them in minutes. Sliced in four wedges and cooked cut-side down, they are at their best eaten the same day, split and buttered while still warm.
sourceNotes: Soda farls are a cornerstone of the Ulster fry and appear in Northern Irish domestic cooking from at least the nineteenth century. Florence White records griddle-cooked soda breads in Good Things in England (1932). The farl (from the Scots Gaelic word meaning fourth part) is cut from a round of dough into quarters before cooking. No oven is needed; the griddle cooks the farl through in under 10 minutes per side.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and cook in two batches on the same griddle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix dry ingredients in advance. Add the buttermilk and cook just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a dry griddle over medium-low heat; no oven required

content.text: Soda farls are ready to eat in 25 minutes from dry ingredients on the shelf. No yeast, no proving, no oven. The bicarbonate of soda reacts with the buttermilk the moment the two meet, so move quickly once the liquid goes in: mix, shape, and get the dough onto a hot griddle within a couple of minutes. Overworking the dough or leaving it to sit will give a tough, flat result.

Sift 250 g plain flour, 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda, and half a teaspoon of salt into a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour in 175 ml buttermilk. Stir quickly with a round-bladed knife or a fork until the dough just comes together into a rough, slightly sticky ball. Do not knead. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and shape into a circle about 1 cm thick and 18 cm across. Cut into four equal wedges.

Heat a heavy cast-iron griddle or dry frying pan over medium-low heat. Do not grease it. When the griddle is hot (a drop of water should skitter across the surface), place the four wedges cut-side down. Cook for 8 to 9 minutes on the first side, until the underside is golden and the dough has risen noticeably at the cut edges. Turn the farls and cook for another 8 to 9 minutes on the second side. The farls are done when they sound hollow when tapped on the base and a skewer through the thickest part comes out clean.
Split each farl open while still warm and butter generously. They are best eaten within an hour of cooking. Leftover farls can be split and toasted cut-side down in a dry pan the next morning.

items.problem: Farls are raw in the middle but burnt on the outside
items.solution: The griddle was too hot. Medium-low is correct: the farls take 8 to 9 minutes per side, allowing the heat to penetrate through without burning the outside. Reduce the heat and be patient.

items.problem: Dough is too wet to handle
items.solution: Buttermilk varies in thickness. Add the liquid gradually, stopping when the dough comes together but is not yet sticky. A small handful of extra flour on the work surface helps with shaping.
